title: Stochastic Path Sampler For Lattice Field Theory
authors: Shiyang Chen; Moxian Qian; Gert Aarts; Biagio Lucini; Kai Zhou
problem: Data-free sampling of an unnormalized lattice target near criticality, where ordinary chains develop long autocorrelation times.
method: Learn paired forward and auxiliary backward Langevin paths by minimizing a path-space variational free energy / entropy-production upper bound.
correction_exactness: Independent path proposals are corrected with an extended-space independence Metropolis-Hastings step; uncorrected SPS is not treated as exact.
result: Corrected SPS observables agree with HMC in the tested 2D phi4 systems and show shorter autocorrelation near the pseudocritical region.
limitation: Uncorrected tails and multimodal regions deviate; finite network capacity, terminal diffusion and support coverage remain practical limits, and the reported timing is hardware/setup specific.

title: Path Integral Sampler: a stochastic control approach for sampling
authors: Zhang, Qinsheng and Chen, Yongxin
problem: Construct an efficient sampler for an unnormalized target by controlling a stochastic path rather than fitting a terminal density alone.
method: Formulate sampling as a Schrodinger-bridge / stochastic optimal-control problem and learn a neural control for a reference diffusion.
correction_exactness: Girsanov path likelihood ratios provide importance weights; weighted estimates retain the path-measure correction.
result: The learned path sampler is competitive with the reported MCMC, SMC and normalizing-flow baselines on its benchmark suite.
limitation: Training overhead, time discretization and a suboptimal learned control can produce weight variance and residual bias if correction is omitted.

title: Denoising Diffusion Samplers
authors: Vargas, Francisco and Grathwohl, Will and Doucet, Arnaud
problem: Sample unnormalized targets with a diffusion process without requiring target-distributed training data.
method: Train a denoising diffusion sampler through a path-space objective and construct tractable forward/reverse trajectory densities.
correction_exactness: Trajectory importance weights correct the learned proposal under the stated discretization; estimator quality depends on weight variance.
result: The method performs strongly on the paper's multimodal and Bayesian benchmarks relative to selected diffusion and SMC baselines.
limitation: Optimization and diffusion discretization are sensitive, and the paper identifies smoothing/objective design as open improvements.

title: Transport meets Variational Inference: Controlled Monte Carlo Diffusions
authors: Vargas, Francisco and Padhy, Shreyas and Blessing, Denis and N \"u sken, Nikolas
problem: Reduce the variance and instability of Monte Carlo diffusion samplers built from a fixed annealing process.
method: Jointly adapt forward and backward controlled diffusions using path-space divergences linked to transport and nonequilibrium identities.
correction_exactness: Path likelihood ratios / importance weights supply the correction; the learned controls target lower-variance paths.
result: Controlled Monte Carlo Diffusions improve the reported sampling metrics across the benchmark suite.
limitation: Performance still depends on annealing schedule, divergence choice, numerical SDE solution and learned-control quality.

title: NETS: A Non-Equilibrium Transport Sampler
authors: Albergo, Michael S. and Vanden-Eijnden, Eric
problem: Annealed importance sampling can have high work/weight variance when the fixed protocol transports poorly between prior and target.
method: NETS augments nonequilibrium Langevin transport with a learned drift chosen to reduce estimator variance while retaining the nonequilibrium identity.
correction_exactness: Jarzynski/AIS-style weights remain in the estimator, so the learned drift improves efficiency rather than replacing correction.
result: The learned transport improves effective sample size on the reported molecular and lattice phi4 examples.
limitation: Drift capacity, protocol, diffusion strength, training cost and remaining weight variance determine practical gains.

title: Numerical determination of the width and shape of the effective string using Stochastic Normalizing Flows
authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
problem: Compute effective-string observables that are difficult to sample accurately with conventional local updates.
method: Use stochastic normalizing flows that interleave invertible layers with stochastic nonequilibrium updates.
correction_exactness: Jarzynski/reweighting factors correct the nonequilibrium proposal.
result: The method reproduces Nambu-Goto benchmarks and is used to study string width, flux-tube shape and beyond-leading behavior.
result_anchor: PDF results and conclusion, around pp.15-20
limitation: This is an effective-string application, not a general scaling demonstration for realistic gauge theories; more complex models remain future work.

title: Stochastic normalizing flows for Effective String Theory
authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
problem: Apply stochastic normalizing flows to effective-string observables with reduced autocorrelation and controlled estimates.
method: Combine normalizing-flow transformations with stochastic updates along an interpolating protocol.
correction_exactness: Nonequilibrium reweighting supplies the correction.
result: The proceedings contribution reports Binder and flux-profile observables consistent with reference expectations.
limitation: Proceedings-scale evidence and one application domain limit conclusions about general scalability.

title: Stochastic normalizing flows for lattice field theory
authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada; Marco Panero
problem: Improve lattice-field sampling by combining learned invertible maps with robust Monte Carlo transitions.
method: Interleave normalizing-flow layers and Monte Carlo updates to form a stochastic normalizing flow for 2D phi4 theory.
correction_exactness: Jarzynski/path weights correct the stochastic transformation.
result: The short study reports improved sampling efficiency over its purely learned or stochastic components.
limitation: The paper is a compact proceedings report in a low-dimensional scalar benchmark, so broad scaling claims are unsupported.

title: Stochastic normalizing flows as non-equilibrium transformations
authors: Michele Caselle; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada; Marco Panero
problem: Normalizing flows can be hard to train globally, while nonequilibrium transformations can require long protocols.
method: Unify invertible flow layers and stochastic nonequilibrium transitions within a path-probability/Jarzynski framework.
correction_exactness: Nonequilibrium work weights preserve free-energy and expectation-value identities.
result: Hybrid stochastic normalizing flows improve free-energy estimation and sampling efficiency in the reported lattice examples.
limitation: Efficiency trades off network training, protocol length, stochastic steps and residual work variance.

title: Physics-Conditioned Diffusion Models for Lattice Gauge Theory
authors: Qianteng Zhu; Gert Aarts; Wei Wang; Kai Zhou; Lingxiao Wang
problem: Generate gauge configurations across couplings while respecting gauge structure and reducing topological freezing.
method: Train a physics-conditioned diffusion model and use annealed Langevin / Metropolis-adjusted Langevin updates in 2D U(1).
correction_exactness: MALA provides an exact target-preserving correction when used; raw diffusion output alone is approximate.
result: The conditioned model transfers across couplings/lattice settings and improves topological exploration in the reported tests.
limitation: Training-data topology can bias the model; non-Abelian groups, fermion nonlocality and large-volume cost remain open.

title: Generative Diffusion Models for Lattice Field Theory
authors: Lingxiao Wang; Gert Aarts; Kai Zhou
problem: Connect diffusion generative models to lattice stochastic quantization and test whether they can serve as global proposals.
method: Learn the reverse diffusion / score process in a toy model and 2D phi4 theory.
correction_exactness: The main generated ensemble is approximate; the paper discusses accept-reject correction as an optional exact step.
result: The model reproduces tested observables and can reduce chain autocorrelation when used as a global sampler.
limitation: The demonstrations are toy/2D, require training configurations, and leave data-free training, gauge fields, fermions and sign problems open.

title: Diffusion model for SU(N) gauge theories
authors: Javad Komijani; Marina K. Marinkovic; Lara Turgut
problem: Build diffusion samplers directly on non-Abelian SU(N) gauge manifolds while preserving gauge symmetry.
method: Use implicit score matching with a gauge-equivariant U-Net and a predictor-corrector/Hamiltonian refinement.
correction_exactness: The corrector reduces residual error, but exactness and cost depend on the implemented target-preserving step.
result: The paper demonstrates 2D and 4D SU(3)-family gauge sampling with improved observables after correction.
limitation: Correction raises computational cost; large-volume/continuum efficiency and richer gauge dynamics remain unresolved.

title: An optimal control perspective on diffusion-based generative modeling
authors: Julius Berner; Lorenz Richter; Karen Ullrich
problem: Understand diffusion generative modeling as an optimal-control problem for unnormalized densities.
method: Derive time-reversed diffusion samplers from Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman control and path-space KL objectives.
correction_exactness: Path likelihoods and the controlled process define the corrected sampling objective; practical exactness depends on numerical implementation.
result: The method outperforms selected diffusion-sampler baselines on the paper's general benchmark suite.
limitation: It is not lattice-specific and leaves high-dimensional physics scaling and numerical-control error to future applications.

title: Operator Spectroscopy of Trained Lattice Samplers
authors: Moxian Qian
problem: Global summary metrics can hide which physical modes a trained lattice sampler fails to learn.
method: Project learned score/force errors onto operator sectors such as zero modes, finite momentum modes and gauge-loop structures.
correction_exactness: Diagnostic only; it does not make an approximate sampler exact.
result: Operator-resolved tests localize sampler error that aggregate loss or ESS can miss.
limitation: The operator basis and interpretation are model- and symmetry-dependent, and a detected failure still needs an algorithmic remedy.

title: Flow-based sampling for fermionic lattice field theories
authors: Michael S. Albergo; Gurtej Kanwar; Sébastien Racanière; Danilo J. Rezende; Julian M. Urban; Denis Boyda; Kyle Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Phiala E. Shanahan
problem: Extend flow-based sampling from bosonic fields to theories with dynamical fermions and nonlocal determinants.
method: Construct joint, marginal and pseudofermion-conditioned flow models and test them in a 2D Yukawa system.
correction_exactness: Reweighting or Metropolis correction retains target exactness for tractable flow densities.
result: The proof of principle reproduces observables in a dynamical-fermion lattice example.
limitation: The study does not establish realistic 4D QCD scaling; pseudofermion variance and determinant/nonlocal costs remain central.

title: Aspects of scaling and scalability for flow-based sampling of lattice QCD
authors: Ryan Abbott; Michael S. Albergo; Aleksandar Botev; Denis Boyda; K. Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Alexander Matthews; Sébastien Racanière; Ali Razavi; Danilo Jimenez Rezende; Fernando Romero-López; Phiala E. Shanahan; Julian M. Urban
problem: Determine whether flow-based lattice-QCD sampling is actually scalable rather than extrapolating from toy-model ESS.
method: Decompose training, proposal and correction costs and analyze how architecture, transfer learning, stopping rules and protocols change scaling.
correction_exactness: Reweighting or independence Metropolis retains exactness, but cost must include training and correction.
result: The paper shows that no single flow scaling law is meaningful; scalability must be assessed experimentally for each full algorithm.
limitation: Training cost can vary by orders of magnitude with protocol, and state-of-the-art QCD viability remains unproven.

title: Sampling SU(3) pure gauge theory with Stochastic Normalizing Flows
authors: Andrea Bulgarelli; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada
problem: Mitigate critical slowing/topological freezing in 4D SU(3) while retaining favorable nonequilibrium scaling.
method: Insert gauge-equivariant coupling layers into a non-equilibrium Monte Carlo protocol and train by dissipated-work/KL reduction.
correction_exactness: Jarzynski/reweighting corrects the final estimator.
result: SNF reaches similar KL/ESS with about half the protocol steps of NE-MCMC and inherits the tested volume scaling.
limitation: Changing all links still implies n_step proportional to volume and becomes very expensive on fine lattices; protocol and architecture optimization remain open.

title: SCORENF: Score-based Normalizing Flows for Sampling Unnormalized distributions
authors: Vikas Kanaujia; Vipul Arora
problem: Avoid mode collapse and mode covering when learning unnormalized multimodal targets from limited data.
method: Train an invertible normalizing flow with score-based and reverse-KL terms, then use it as an IMH proposal.
correction_exactness: IMH gives asymptotically unbiased target samples; the learned flow alone can retain support errors.
result: ScoreNF improves the reported NLL/RNLL balance, ESS and acceptance on Gaussian mixtures and a 64-dimensional phi4 example.
limitation: It still relies on target samples for score estimation and is only tested on modest systems; larger symmetric systems are future work.

title: Diffusion Models for Sampling Near Criticality in Lattice Field Theories
authors: Yang-yang Tan; Gert Aarts; Diaa E. Habibi; Biagio Lucini; Lingxiao Wang
problem: Test diffusion sampling near criticality and whether training on cheap small volumes transfers to unseen large lattices.
method: Use a fully convolutional reverse-SDE score model trained across volumes, plus score, MALA-acceptance and HMC-referenced ESS diagnostics.
correction_exactness: Raw diffusion ensembles retain residual bias; an exact composite MALA chain can remove it.
result: Cross-volume models reproduce most 2D/3D phi4 observables and the propagator at unseen sizes, including L=64, with strongest residuals in infrared/zero-mode quantities.
limitation: Results use scalar theories, support filters and 2000 score evaluations; zero-mode/action-density bias, transfer range, gauge equivariance and fermions remain open.

title: Scalable Generative Sampling and Multilevel Estimation for Lattice Field Theories Near Criticality
authors: A. Singha; J. Kauffmann; E. Cellini; K. Jansen; S. Nakajima
problem: Overcome critical slowing and poor large-volume scaling by representing long- and short-distance modes separately.
method: Factor the target coarse-to-fine; use conditional Gaussian mixtures and masked continuous normalizing flows while preserving coarse sites exactly.
correction_exactness: Importance sampling corrects the learned density; exact restriction enables an unbiased MLMC estimator.
result: On critical 2D phi4, the method reports much lower autocorrelation than HMC, high IS efficiency, consistent observables and MLMC variance reduction.
limitation: ESS still falls at the largest volumes, fine-level quality limits MLMC gains, and extension to group-valued gauge theories is open.

title: Variational Autoregressive Networks Applied to phi4 Field Theory Systems
authors: Moxian Qian; Shiyang Chen
problem: Build data-free autoregressive samplers for discrete and continuous lattice systems and reduce residual proposal bias.
method: Train variational autoregressive networks, add single-site/block MH corrections, and use transfer learning across coupling and size.
correction_exactness: MH corrections target the desired distribution; pure VAN output can remain biased.
result: VAN+MH agrees with HMC on the tested Ising/phi4 systems and transfer learning reduces training epochs in the reported ranges.
limitation: Only small lattices/parameter ranges are tested; gauge theories, larger finite-size scaling and other correction mechanisms remain open.

title: Diffusion models as stochastic quantization in lattice field theory
authors: Wang, Lingxiao and Aarts, Gert and Zhou, Kai
problem: Relate modern denoising diffusion to stochastic quantization and use it to reduce lattice-chain autocorrelation.
method: Learn a reverse Langevin/score process from HMC data and deploy it as a global proposal in 2D phi4.
correction_exactness: An accept-reject step can be added; uncorrected DM samples are approximate.
result: The trained model reproduces tested configurations and reduces autocorrelation in symmetric and broken phases.
limitation: The study is a simple 2D demonstration and leaves data-free training, non-Abelian fields, fermions and complex actions unresolved.

title: Flow-based generative models for Markov chain Monte Carlo in lattice field theory
authors: Albergo, M. S. and Kanwar, G. and Shanahan, P. E.
problem: Critical slowing makes conventional lattice MCMC expensive near phase transitions.
method: Train a normalizing flow without target samples and use independent flow proposals in a Metropolis-Hastings chain.
correction_exactness: Metropolis-Hastings guarantees asymptotic convergence if support/ergodicity conditions hold.
result: In 2D phi4, trained flow proposals reproduce observables and can eliminate sampling-stage critical slowing at fixed acceptance.
limitation: Training took substantial GPU time in this proof of principle; architecture scaling, memory and extension to QCD were not established.

title: Equivariant flow-based sampling for lattice gauge theory
authors: Gurtej Kanwar; Michael S. Albergo; Denis Boyda; K. Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Sébastien Racanière; Danilo Jimenez Rezende; Phiala E. Shanahan
problem: Topological freezing makes gauge-field MCMC inefficient toward the continuum limit.
method: Construct gauge-equivariant normalizing-flow coupling layers and use them as independence-Metropolis proposals in 2D U(1).
correction_exactness: Metropolis correction makes estimates asymptotically exact.
result: For the tested beta=7, 16x16 setup, topological autocorrelation is far smaller than HMC/heat bath and the paper reports large efficiency gains for topological observables.
limitation: This is an Abelian 2D proof of principle; expressive invertible kernels for non-Abelian theories and full scaling remained future work.

title: Flow-based sampling for multimodal and extended-mode distributions in lattice field theory
authors: Daniel C. Hackett and Chung-Chun Hsieh and Sahil Pontula and Michael S. Albergo and Denis Boyda and Jiunn-Wei Chen and Kai-Feng Chen and Kyle Cranmer and Gurtej Kanwar and Phiala E. Shanahan
problem: Reverse-KL flow training can collapse modes or miss low-density inner tails even when global metrics look good.
method: Develop topology matching, equivariant/mixture architectures, forward-KL/adaptive retraining and composite HMC+flow sampling.
correction_exactness: Composite HMC steps regulate independence-MH pathologies; target-distributed diagnostics expose missed tails.
result: The tested methods alleviate mode collapse in real and complex scalar theories, while hybrid chains escape high-weight tails more reliably.
limitation: Inner-tail mismodeling persists, metrics can be deceptive, and architecture/training must be specialized; comparative cost is application dependent.

title: Machine learning for four-dimensional SU(3) lattice gauge theories
authors: Urs Wenger
problem: Assess which machine-learning routes have credible prospects for 4D SU(3) lattice gauge theory.
method: Review normalizing/stochastic flows, diffusion processes and learned renormalization-group/fixed-point actions with physics constraints.
correction_exactness: Exactness depends on each underlying sampler/correction; the article is a review, not a new universal correction.
result: The review identifies physics-conditioned diffusion, stochastic nonequilibrium flows and learned fixed-point actions as promising directions with some 4D evidence.
limitation: Most approaches remain exploratory; plain normalizing flows have not scaled simultaneously to 4D, large volume and fine spacing, and physics input appears essential.

title: Sampling QCD field configurations with gauge-equivariant flow models
authors: Ryan Abbott; Michael S. Albergo; Aleksandar Botev; Denis Boyda; K. Cranmer; Daniel C. Hackett; Gurtej Kanwar; Alexander Matthews; Sébastien Racanière; Ali Razavi; Danilo Jimenez Rezende; Fernando Romero-López; Phiala E. Shanahan; Julian M. Urban
problem: Test whether gauge-equivariant flows can be assembled for dynamical-fermion QCD rather than only toy gauge systems.
method: Combine marginal gauge and conditional pseudofermion flow models for a 4D two-flavor QCD demonstration.
correction_exactness: Reweighting or independence Metropolis corrects model proposals.
result: On a 4^4 lattice the reweighted flow ensemble is statistically consistent with HMC for plaquette, Polyakov loop, pion correlator and topology.
limitation: The lattice is tiny; model/training choices and scaling tradeoffs are largely unexplored, and at-scale use requires major engineering/HPC integration.

title: A scalable flow-based approach to mitigate topological freezing
authors: Claudio Bonanno; Andrea Bulgarelli; Elia Cellini; Alessandro Nada; Dario Panfalone; Davide Vadacchino; Lorenzo Verzichelli
problem: Use fast open-boundary topology dynamics while recovering periodic-boundary observables without boundary artifacts.
method: Train localized gauge-equivariant defect SNFs that interpolate OBC to PBC through nonequilibrium updates.
correction_exactness: Jarzynski reweighting restores periodic-boundary expectation values exactly in the estimator.
result: In tested 4D SU(3), defect SNF improves over NE-MCMC by about a factor of three at fixed estimator quality and reproduces topological susceptibility references.
limitation: Richer layers, optimized schedules and dynamical fermions remain open; the factor is setup specific.

title: Group-Equivariant Diffusion Models for Lattice Field Theory
authors: Vega, Octavio and Komijani, Javad and El-Khadra, Aida and Marinkovic, Marina
problem: Improve diffusion sample quality for scalar and gauge targets by encoding exact symmetries and target forces.
method: Use group-equivariant score networks with force-regularized score matching for 2D phi4 and U(1).
correction_exactness: Reported observables are reweighted; MALA is identified as an alternative exact refinement.
result: Symmetry-aware models outperform generic scores and reproduce tested observables with ESS reported between 15% and 65% in U(1) examples.
limitation: ESS decreases with volume, sampling is computationally expensive, and higher dimensions, SU(3), fermions and integrator choices remain untested.

title: Sampling U(1) gauge theory using a re-trainable conditional flow-based model
authors: Ankur Singha; Dipankar Chakrabarti; Arora, Vipul
problem: Extrapolate a lattice gauge sampler to couplings where HMC topology freezes without retraining from scratch at every point.
method: Train a beta-conditioned normalizing flow on HMC ensembles, use MH, and retrain with intermediate model samples for farther extrapolation.
correction_exactness: Metropolis-Hastings corrects conditional-flow proposals.
result: In 2D U(1), extrapolated proposals retain roughly 50-60% acceptance over the reported range and reduce topological freezing.
limitation: Acceptance falls with extrapolation distance and the authors state the conditional flow is generally not scalable to high-dimensional targets.

title: The critical slowing down in diffusion models
authors: Luca Maria Del Bono; Giulio Biroli; Patrick Charbonneau; Marylou Gabrie
problem: Determine whether diffusion models automatically escape critical slowing down in training and generation.
method: Analyze score training and reverse generation exactly in the Gaussian O(n to infinity) model; compare one-layer, two-layer and local score architectures.
correction_exactness: No target-correction mechanism is proposed; this is a controlled theory/architecture analysis.
result: A one-layer exact-form score learns with critical slowing and transmits it to generation; a two-layer/local architecture changes training scaling from quadratic to logarithmic in system size in the analyzed setting.
limitation: The result is controlled only in the Gaussian O(n to infinity) limit; deeper architectures add overhead and finite-n/non-equilibrium systems remain open.
